title: angelica dahurica
intro: Angelica dahurica, also known as Dahurian angelica or Baituangelica, is a plant species in the carrot family (Apiaceae) native to East Asia. It has been used in traditional medicine for its medicinal properties, including antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral activities, which contribute to its health benefits such as reducing fever, relieving pain, and promoting digestion. The plant is often prepared into herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being used in combination with other herbs in traditional Chinese medicine. Additionally, the plant's roots and rhizomes can be dried and used as a spice or added to soups for its flavor and medicinal properties.
benefits_desc: Angelica dahurica is used to treat a variety of health issues, including digestive problems, respiratory issues, and skin conditions. It has been traditionally used to reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms of the common cold, flu, and bronchitis. The plant is also believed to have antiseptic properties, making it effective in treating wounds and preventing infections. By consuming angelica dahurica, individuals can potentially benefit from improved circulation, reduced fever, and enhanced immune function, as well as relief from headaches, nausea, and menstrual cramps.
actions_desc: Angelica dahurica has demonstrated a range of therapeutic actions, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, which primarily affect the respiratory, cardiovascular, and digestive systems. Additionally, it exhibits sedative, nervine, and antispasmodic effects, influencing the nervous system and providing relief from anxiety and muscle tension. This plant also possesses antioxidant, immunomodulatory, and anti-cancer properties, impacting various body systems including the immune, endocrine, and oncological systems. Furthermore, it exhibits diuretic, expectorant, and anti-inflammatory actions, affecting the urinary, respiratory, and musculoskeletal systems.

actions_list.name: antiseptic
actions_list.desc: has antimicrobial properties, preventing the growth of microorganisms on the skin and in wounds.

actions_list.name: anti-inflammatory
actions_list.desc: reduces inflammation and swelling by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes.

actions_list.name: expectorant
actions_list.desc: helps to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, making it easier to cough up.

actions_list.name: antimicrobial
actions_list.desc: has broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, effective against bacteria, viruses, and fungi.

actions_list.name: antispasmodic
actions_list.desc: relaxes smooth muscle spasms, reducing cramping and pain in the digestive tract and other areas.

actions_list.name: tonic
actions_list.desc: has a general strengthening effect on the body, improving overall health and well-being.

actions_list.name: uterotonic
actions_list.desc: stimulates the uterus to contract, helping to prevent or treat postpartum hemorrhage and uterine atony.

constituents_desc: Angelica dahurica has been found to contain a diverse array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin, which possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties to protect against oxidative stress and cell damage. The plant also contains lignans like sesamin and sesamolin, which exhibit antimicrobial and immunomodulatory effects to support immune function and prevent infections. Additionally, Angelica dahurica is rich in coumarins, including scopoletin and umbelliferone, which display anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic activities to alleviate pain and muscle tension. Furthermore, the plant's bioactive constituents also include alkaloids, glycosides, and sesquiterpenes, showcasing its potential for broad-spectrum medicinal applications.

constituents_list.name: sesquiterpene

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Angelica dahurica for health purposes are the roots, stems, leaves, and flowers. The roots are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, such as nausea and vomiting, as well as to reduce inflammation and improve circulation. The leaves and stems are used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, and to reduce fever, while the flowers are used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The plant's various parts are also used to treat other ailments, including menstrual cramps, indigestion, and skin conditions, making it a versatile herb in traditional medicine.

parts_list.name: leaves
parts_list.desc: the leaves of the angelica dahurica plant are alternate, pinnate, and have 3-5 pairs of leaflets. they are ovate-lanceolate in shape and have a pointed tip.

parts_list.name: stem
parts_list.desc: the stem of the angelica dahurica plant is stout, glabrous, and has a few branches. it can grow up to 1-2 meters in height.

parts_list.name: root
parts_list.desc: the root of the angelica dahurica plant is thick, fleshy, and fibrous. it is the part of the plant that is used for medicinal purposes.

parts_list.name: rhizome
parts_list.desc: the rhizome of the angelica dahurica plant is a thick, underground stem that produces new shoots and roots. it is used for medicinal purposes.

parts_list.name: flowers
parts_list.desc: the flowers of the angelica dahurica plant are small, greenish-white, and clustered in umbrella-like structures. they are wind-pollinated.

parts_list.name: seeds
parts_list.desc: the seeds of the angelica dahurica plant are small, brown, and contained within the fruit. they are not typically used for medicinal purposes.

parts_list.name: fruit
parts_list.desc: the fruit of the angelica dahurica plant is a small, greenish-yellow berry that contains several seeds. it is not typically used for medicinal purposes.

preparations_desc: Angelica dahurica most common herbal preparations are infusions, decoctions, and tinctures. Infusions are used to treat fever, sore throats, and respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma, while decoctions are used to alleviate digestive problems like bloating and indigestion. Tinctures are also used to treat various ailments, including coughs, colds, and skin irritations like eczema and acne. In addition, these preparations can also be used to reduce inflammation, alleviate menstrual cramps, and boost the immune system.

preparations_list.name: dahurian angelica infusion
preparations_list.desc: a herbal infusion made by steeping the roots of the angelica dahurica plant in hot water, traditionally used to aid digestion and relieve colds.

preparations_list.name: angelica dahurica tincture
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated liquid extract of the roots of the angelica dahurica plant, often used to treat respiratory issues and as a natural expectorant.

preparations_list.name: angelica dahurica essential oil
preparations_list.desc: a volatile oil extracted from the roots and rhizomes of the angelica dahurica plant, used in aromatherapy to promote relaxation and reduce stress.

preparations_list.name: dahurian angelica decoction
preparations_list.desc: a longer simmered herbal tea made by combining the roots of the angelica dahurica plant with other herbs, traditionally used to treat fever and alleviate coughs.

preparations_list.name: angelica dahurica salve
preparations_list.desc: a topical preparation made by infusing the roots of the angelica dahurica plant in a carrier oil, often used to soothe skin irritations and reduce inflammation.

preparations_list.name: dahurian angelica poultice
preparations_list.desc: a topical application made by crushing the roots of the angelica dahurica plant and applying it directly to the skin, traditionally used to treat boils and abscesses.

preparations_list.name: angelica dahurica capsules
preparations_list.desc: a standardized herbal supplement made by encapsulating the dried roots of the angelica dahurica plant, often used to support immune function and reduce inflammation.

side_effects_desc: Angelica dahurica side effects are commonly experienced by individuals who consume it in large quantities or for an extended period, particularly those with pre-existing liver conditions or allergies. Common side effects include dizziness, headaches, and nausea, as well as more severe reactions such as liver damage, irregular heartbeat, and allergic skin reactions like hives and itching. Individuals with kidney problems or taking medications like blood thinners, diuretics, or antacids may be more likely to experience these side effects, which can also include stomach ulcers, diarrhea, and interactions with certain vitamins and minerals like vitamin K and iron. Precautions include taking Angelica dahurica in moderation, avoiding it during pregnancy and breastfeeding, and being aware of the potential interactions with other substances, such as alcohol and certain herbs, to minimize the risk of adverse reactions.

side_effects_list.name: allergic reactions
side_effects_list.desc: some people may experience allergic reactions, such as hives, itching, or difficulty breathing, after consuming or touching the angelica dahurica plant.

side_effects_list.name: digestive issues
side_effects_list.desc: consuming large amounts of the angelica dahurica plant may cause digestive issues, including nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.

side_effects_list.name: skin irritation
side_effects_list.desc: the plant's sap or oils may cause skin irritation, such as redness, itching, or rashes, especially if not handled properly.

side_effects_list.name: interactions with medications
side_effects_list.desc: the angelica dahurica plant may interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, and decrease their effectiveness or increase the risk of bleeding.

side_effects_list.name: liver damage
side_effects_list.desc: consuming large amounts of the plant over an extended period may cause liver damage or increase the risk of liver disease.

side_effects_list.name: respiratory problems
side_effects_list.desc: inhaling the plant's oils or sap may cause respiratory problems, such as coughing, wheezing, or shortness of breath.

side_effects_list.name: seizures or convulsions
side_effects_list.desc: in rare cases, consuming the angelica dahurica plant may cause seizures or convulsions, especially in people with a history of seizure disorders.

benefits_list.name: antimicrobial properties
benefits_list.desc: the angelica dahurica plant has been found to exhibit antimicrobial properties, which can help prevent the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms.

benefits_list.name: anti-inflammatory effects
benefits_list.desc: the plant has been traditionally used to treat various inflammatory conditions, such as fever, rheumatism, and gout.

benefits_list.name: digestive health support
benefits_list.desc: angelica dahurica is believed to aid in digestion, relieve symptoms of indigestion, and prevent diarrhea.

benefits_list.name: antiviral activity
benefits_list.desc: the plant has been found to exhibit antiviral properties, which can help prevent the replication of certain viruses.

benefits_list.name: respiratory system support
benefits_list.desc: angelica dahurica is traditionally used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis, coughs, and colds.

benefits_list.name: antioxidant properties
benefits_list.desc: the plant contains antioxidants that can help protect the body from oxidative stress and cell damage.

benefits_list.name: pain relief
benefits_list.desc: angelica dahurica has been traditionally used to relieve pain and reduce fever.
